God’s withdrawings are very distressing to his people, especially during times of trouble. We distance ourselves from God through our lack of faith, and then complain that God distances himself from us. Strong words against bad people do more harm than good; if we speak about their wickedness, let it be to the Lord in prayer; he can make them better. The sinner proudly boasts in his power and success. Wicked people will not seek after God, that is, they will not call upon him. They live without prayer, and that is living without God. They have many thoughts, many objects and plans, but do not think of the Lord in any of them; they have no submission to his will, nor aim for his glory. The cause of this is pride. People think it beneath them to be religious. They could not break all the laws of justice and goodness toward people if they had not first shaken off all sense of religion.

The psalmist speaks with astonishment at the wickedness of the wicked, and at God’s patience and forbearance. God prepares the heart for prayer by kindling holy desires and strengthening our most holy faith, focusing the thoughts, and raising the affections, and then he graciously accepts the prayer. The preparation of the heart is from the Lord, and we must seek it from him. Let the poor, afflicted, persecuted, or tempted believer remember that Satan is the prince of this world, and that he is the father of all the ungodly. The children of God cannot expect kindness, truth, or justice from people who crucified the Lord of glory. But Jesus, who once suffered, now reigns as King over all the earth, and his dominion will have no end. Let us commit ourselves to him, humbly trusting in his mercy. He will rescue the believer from every temptation, break the arm of every wicked oppressor, and bruise Satan under our feet shortly. But in heaven alone will all sin and temptation be shut out, though in this life the believer has a foretaste of deliverance.
